data.body:
=== RESEARCH REPORT ===
=== Opus: CRITICAL design review of v3 (pressure-test)
=== agent a7a38abaa5b014714

I've read both documents fully. This is a dense, well-researched design. Let me deliver a prioritized adversarial review.

---

# CRITICAL DESIGN REVIEW: rebar plan-review gate (v3)

The authors have done genuinely impressive research. That research is also the source of the design's central failure: it has been used to *justify a big system* rather than to *bound the smallest one that works*. The cited evidence repeatedly argues against this design's own shape, and the authors have absorbed those citations as caveats rather than as redesign forces. Below, most-damaging first.

---

## FINDING 1 (CRITICAL) — The design's own evidence says an LLM plan-gate is dominated by false-ACCEPTANCE, yet the entire build is engineered against false-positives

This is the most damaging finding because it undermines the premise, not the implementation.

§10C cites Kambhampati (2310.08118): GPT-4 self-verifying Blocksworld plans **wrongly ACCEPTED 84.45% of invalid plans; false-reject was only 1.8%.** §10D's nine "uncovered failure modes" (fault-localization #1 SWE-bench failure, missing-verification ~21-23%, slopsquatting) are all *misses*, i.e. false-negatives. The verdict model (§1, §10B) makes **FAIL the safe bottom that blocks**, and the anti-FP contract (§1) is six interlocking mechanisms (binary checklists, missing/wrong routing, benign-reading filter, calibrated abstention, coherence pass, anti-fabrication) — *all aimed at suppressing false-positives.*

So the design pours its engineering into the failure mode its own evidence says is **rare (1.8%)** and structurally under-defends the one its evidence says **dominates (84%)**. The benign-reading filter, the calibrated-abstention threshold, the "graduate to block only once FPR clears the bar" policy — every one of these *lowers* the gate's willingness to flag, pushing an already-too-lenient judge further toward rubber-stamping. You will ship a gate that passes bad plans confidently and occasionally annoys good ones, then tune it to annoy good ones even less.

**Why it matters:** The gate's entire reason to exist is catching flawed plans. The evidence says LLM judges are *bad at exactly that* and *good at the thing you're spending all your complexity suppressing.* You're optimizing the wrong loss.

**Recommendation (change):** Re-anchor the whole contract around false-negatives. (a) The mechanisms that genuinely earn keep are the **sound/deterministic** ones (they don't share the LLM's acceptance bias). (b) For judgment dims, abstention should default toward *escalate/surface*, not *drop* — the asymmetric cost runs the opposite direction from how §1 tuned it. (c) Kill the "advise-first, graduate-to-block on low FPR" path for the dims where the danger is misses; low measured FPR on a lenient judge is not evidence of correctness, it's evidence of silence. Track FNR with a **deliberately seeded set of known-bad plans** as the primary metric, not FPR.

---

## FINDING 2 (CRITICAL) — The full pipeline cost/latency is economically incoherent for an every-pickup gate, and §10E already knows it

§10E lays out the correct architecture in one sentence: *deterministic prefilter (≈free) → Haiku (~$0.005) on residual → Opus (~$0.025) only on low confidence; condition on difficulty, don't fire flat; cost scales with RESIDUAL fraction.* Then §3 specifies the opposite: a flat fan-out of **~9 Tier-1 evals + ~5 Tier-2 agents + router + N triggered overlays (up to 9 in T1–T9, several multi-part) + coherence pass + benign-team adjudicator + verdict aggregation**, *per plan.*

Tier-2 and the overlays are **agents** (multi-turn, tool-using: forced codebase probes, consumer/reference lookups, web search in T1). That's not $0.005-of-Haiku territory; a single tool-using Opus agent doing real codebase probes is dollars and tens of seconds to minutes, and you've specced ~5 of them plus triggered overlays. Realistic per-plan cost is **single-digit-to-low-tens of dollars and multiple minutes of wall-clock**, and the design even notes the registry is a "growth substrate" — i.e. this only goes up.

The "`claim` just checks a signature, heavy review is async" framing **hides but does not solve** this. Someone pays per plan, every plan, every revision (the review-process loop means *re-running the whole thing* after each client revision). For a feature dropped into "diverse client projects," that is a per-ticket tax most teams will switch off the first month.

**Why it matters:** §10E's own cite (Snell 2408.03314) says conditioning on difficulty is >4× more efficient. The design quotes this and then ignores it in §3, which fires the full battery flat.

**Recommendation (cut + change):** Make the cascade real, not aspirational. DET prefilter → **one** Haiku holistic pass with the binary checklist → escalate to Opus *only* on low-confidence/high-difficulty residual → fire overlays *only* when the router has a concrete trigger AND the plan cleared a difficulty threshold. Budget-cap per plan and surface the cap. If you can't state a p50 and p95 dollar+latency number for a plan, you can't ship this as a gate.

---

## FINDING 3 (CRITICAL) — Fail-open silently guts the gate for exactly the clients who need it most, and the design half-admits this in a way that reads as honest but isn't

§2 makes fail-open an "IRONCLAD invariant": unsupported language / no server / crash / timeout → `skipped` = PASS. Hard "missing" only from a top-tier server (pyright/gopls/rust-analyzer/tsserver/clangd). The prompt's worry is correct and the design does not actually resolve it.

For a Terraform / Elixir / COBOL / Zig / Nix client, the entire Tier-0 deterministic spine (P2 resolution, P3 deps, P4/G5 size, P5 DAG via symbols) **degrades to `skipped`=PASS**. What remains is the judgment tiers — the very LLM judge that §10C says false-accepts 84% of bad plans, now running *without* the sound priors that §10E insists are the only trustworthy grounding ("ground in SOUND external signal, not introspection"). So a Go shop gets a real gate; a Terraform shop gets an ungrounded LLM rubber stamp that **says "ready" with a signed attestation that looks identical.** That signal-identical PASS is the dishonest part: the attestation doesn't encode "we actually had grounding for this."

The design is honest *that* fail-open happens; it is **not** honest that the resulting attestation is far weaker for non-mainstream clients while presenting the same green checkmark. And the catalog (P3 LLM-fallback for package existence, P6 polyglot fix) shows the team is already papering cross-language gaps with *more LLM*, which compounds Finding 1.

**Why it matters:** "Used by a broad, diverse client base" is the framing sentence of the whole doc. The gate is weakest precisely where the framing claims it's most needed, and nothing in the attestation tells the client that.

**Recommendation (change):** The attestation MUST carry a **grounding-coverage field** ("N/M sound checks ran; rest skipped-unsupported"). A PASS with zero sound coverage is a *different verdict* (e.g. `ADVISORY-ONLY`) and the gate config should let a project decide whether ungrounded judgment can mint a blocking-grade signature at all. Be honest in the docs: "on unsupported stacks this is an LLM advisory, not a grounded gate."

---

## FINDING 4 (HIGH) — The 30+ dimension / 4-tier registry is gold-plating; the research says the field ships a single holistic pass, and the design overrides that on a thin rationale

§0 itself flags this: *"per-dimension registry + generic orchestrator is UNIQUE in the field (surveyed tools are monolithic-holistic single prompts or hard-coded persona panels) — it's a real bet."* The catalog (Part 4C/4D) shows even DSO's *own* plan-review is a **4-criterion single pass** (Feasibility/Completeness/YAGNI/Codebase-Alignment, PASS/REVISE). The big dimension counts in the catalog are DSO's *authoring* pipeline (brainstorm/preplanning), not its plan gate.

Being "unique in the field" for a reviewer architecture is not a selling point — it's a warning that everyone who tried converged elsewhere. The per-dimension approach buys *actionability* and *low cross-talk*, but at the cost of (a) N× the LLM calls (Finding 2), (b) **guaranteed double-flagging** despite the coherence pass, and (c) a coherence/aggregation layer whose only job is to undo the fragmentation you introduced.

Concrete redundancies that *will* double-flag the same plan defect:
- **E1 (criteria↔description coherence) vs E6 (end-state reachability) vs G3 (child coverage)** — all three fire on "the steps/children don't add up to the stated AC." One missing-coverage defect, three findings.
- **F1 (measurability/completability) vs E2 (ambiguity/executable-without-clarification)** — a vague AC trips both.
- **P4 (oversize signals) vs G5 (decomposition judgment, which "consumes P4")** — overlapping by the design's own admission.
- **A1 (over-engineering/NIH/config-proliferation) vs T1 (prior-art/anti-repackaging/Rule-of-Three)** — Rule-of-Three appears in *both*. NIH ≈ anti-repackaging.
- **T4 (compat/destructiveness) vs P7/O4 (destructive sniff) vs T5b (reliability/blast-radius)** — destructive-change concern spread across three places.
- **T5c security "ambiguity-not-absence" vs E2 ambiguity** — security ambiguity is ambiguity.

Dimensions that don't earn their keep in a *plan* (pre-execution) gate: **T5d Accessibility, T6 UX non-happy-path, T7 Documentation excessiveness/navigability, T8 LLM/prompt antipatterns.** These are code/design-review concerns; you cannot meaningfully assess accessibility or doc-navigability from a *plan*, and trying will manufacture false positives (Finding 1's other tail).

**Recommendation (cut/merge):** Collapse to a single holistic judgment pass with a **structured rubric** of ~6 grouped concerns (Coherence-of-coverage, Measurability/clarity, Scope/decomposition, Assumption-grounding, Testing/verification, Destructiveness) — get the actionability via *structured output sections*, not separate LLM invocations. Keep the registry as a **config seam for the deterministic checks** (which genuinely are independent and cheap) and for the *one* future case the design actually names (project-invariant compliance). Defer the per-judgment-dimension plurality until you have data showing the holistic pass *misses* something a split would catch.

---

## FINDING 5 (HIGH) — Calibrated abstention requires labeled data that won't exist, and the design knows the data problem but not its consequence

§1 specifies "calibrated abstention: confidence per finding; surface only above a threshold calibrated on a small human gold set (N≈5-10)." §10B then *correctly* demolishes this: abstention must be a *calibrated error-ranking signal (multi-sample agreement / self-consistency / ensemble) validated on labeled data — NOT raw verbalized judge confidence (AUROC ~0.55)*, and cites AbstentionBench (2506.09038): **abstention is unsolved; reasoning-FT degrades it.**

Two fatal problems the design doesn't reconcile:
1. **N≈5-10 is not a calibration set, it's a rounding error.** You cannot calibrate a per-dimension threshold for 30 dimensions across *arbitrary client languages and domains* from 5-10 hand-labeled plans. Per-dim FPR/FNR calibration (listed as cross-cutting work) needs hundreds of labeled examples *per dimension per domain* to mean anything. That data does not exist and the client base is too heterogeneous to ever pool it.
2. **Multi-sample/self-consistency abstention (what §10B says you actually need) is N× *more* expensive** — it multiplies Finding 2's cost by the sample count.

So the anti-FP contract's keystone (calibrated abstention) is either (a) implemented as verbalized confidence, which the design's own cite says doesn't work (AUROC 0.55 ≈ coin flip), or (b) implemented properly via ensembling, which blows the cost budget. There's no middle.

**Recommendation (de-risk):** Drop per-dimension calibrated abstention from the one-wave scope entirely. Ship a single global confidence gate with a fixed conservative threshold, and **default-to-surface on uncertainty** (consistent with Finding 1). Build the labeled-gold-set infrastructure as a separate, explicit, ongoing track — not a checkbox in a shipping wave — and be honest that calibration is a multi-quarter data problem, not a config value.

---

## FINDING 6 (HIGH) — "Agent-completable-in-session" acceptance criteria is the gate's hardest judgment and it's specified in one clause with no operational definition

The framing (lines 12-13) makes this a **hard constraint**: "Acceptance criteria must be AGENT-COMPLETABLE-IN-SESSION (external ops OK with provided auth; NO real-user/long-wait/host-live validation)." F1 carries it ("+ context-window-fit framing"). This is arguably the *single most valuable* thing a plan gate could check — and it's the least specified.

What is "a session"? Token budget? Wall-clock? Tool-call count? Which agent's session — the reviewer has no idea what model/harness will execute the plan, so "completable-in-session" is undefined without an execution profile the gate doesn't have. "Context-window-fit framing" is hand-waved. This check will be either inert (passes everything) or arbitrary (flags based on the reviewer's guess about an executor it can't see) — and given Finding 1, it'll be inert.

**Why it matters:** It's load-bearing in the framing and unbuildable as written. An undefined hard constraint is a coin-flip flag generator — the worst kind for trust.

**Recommendation (specify or cut):** Either define it operationally (the gate is told the executor's budget envelope and checks the plan's step-count/file-impact/AC-count against thresholds — deterministic, cheap, polyglot) or drop the "in-session" qualifier and check only the verifiable part ("AC is testable by a command, not by a human or a multi-day wait"). Don't ship an undefined hard gate.

---

## FINDING 7 (MEDIUM) — Attestation-invalidation-on-edit + the review-process loop create a re-review treadmill the design hasn't costed

§3/§4 specify "attestation signing + invalidation-on-edit." Combined with "it is a REVIEW PROCESS, not a dialog — the client revises + re-runs to earn a fresh signature," this means: **every edit to a ticket invalidates the signature and forces a full re-review.** Tickets get edited constantly in normal workflow (clarifying a comment, fixing a typo in AC, adding a link). Each edit → invalidated attestation → claim blocked → full pipeline re-run (Finding 2's cost) → wait → claim again.

The catalog explicitly flags DSO's analog as an anti-pattern to avoid ("passed written even on REVISE," forgeable, repo-scoped) — but the *invalidation* side has the opposite failure: too eager. There's no notion of *material* vs *immaterial* edit. §10E mentions "material-event-commit binding" but §3 just says "invalidation-on-edit."

**Recommendation (specify):** Define material-edit scope precisely — only edits to description/AC/file-impact/decomposition invalidate; tags/comments/links/assignee don't. Without this, the gate makes ticket editing radioactive and clients will route around it.

---

## FINDING 8 (MEDIUM) — Implementability: the riskiest pieces are the cross-cutting LLM glue, and they're the least specified

Asked what's most likely to fail to build in one wave:

- **The router-LLM (§3 cross-cutting, "DET priors; recall-biased")** gates which overlays fire. It's an LLM classifier with no spec for its training/prompting, no accuracy target, and it sits on the critical path of cost (wrong router → fires everything or misses the relevant overlay). Recall-bias means it over-fires → Finding 2.
- **The coherence pass (§1)** is described as "single structured check, not debate" for contradictions *between* sections — but cross-section contradiction detection is precisely the hard reasoning task LLMs are worst at, and there's zero spec for how it enumerates section-pairs or what "contradiction" means operationally.
- **Per-dim FPR/FNR calibration** (Finding 5) is listed as one cross-cutting bullet — it's actually a research program.
- **The generic orchestrator** that "never names a dimension" and drives DET/1-TURN/2-STEP/AGENT exec kinds with per-dim trigger/routing/severity/block policies is a small workflow engine. Reusing `rebar.llm`'s catalog.json + contracts + runner is plausible (the catalog confirms those exist), but the *exec-kind dispatch* (especially the AGENT kind with tool-priming from DET results) is the integration nobody has built and it's where one-wave schedules die.

The **deterministic tier (§2) is the genuinely buildable, valuable, low-risk part** — solidlsp is in-repo, syft/scc/lizard are off-the-shelf, fail-open is a clear contract. That's the part I'd ship with confidence.

**Recommendation (de-risk):** Build and ship the deterministic tier + ONE holistic Haiku/Opus-cascade pass first as the actual wave. Treat router, coherence-pass, per-dim calibration, and the AGENT-exec orchestrator as *separate* subsequent work with their own acceptance criteria — not folded into "one wave."

---

## What I'd actually build (the defensible version)

A two-stage gate, not a four-tier battery:

1. **Deterministic prefilter (ship as-is from §2/Tier-0).** P1 shape, P2 resolution, P3 package existence, P5 DAG validity, P4 oversize, plus §10D's cheap structural adds (U9 token-count flag, O4 always-on destructive sniff against an allowlist). Fail-open, but the attestation records *coverage* (Finding 3). These are sound, cheap, polyglot-where-tooling-exists, and they block hard. This is the gate's real spine.

2. **One holistic LLM judgment pass** over the residual, conditioned on difficulty (skip it for trivially-small/well-grounded plans — Snell). Structured-output rubric of ~6 grouped concerns (coverage-coherence, clarity/measurability, scope/decomposition, assumption-grounding-vs-codebase, testing/verification-presence, destructiveness). **Tuned for recall, not precision** (Finding 1): default to surface, escalate to a second Opus pass only on the residual's hardest cases. Findings are **generative** (feed the revise loop), and only the deterministic/sound failures *block*; LLM findings are `ADVISORY` unless a project opts a specific concern into blocking *and* has grounding coverage for it.

3. **Attestation** carries verdict + **grounding-coverage** + material-event binding; invalidates only on material edits (Finding 7). On zero-sound-coverage stacks the verdict is explicitly `ADVISORY-ONLY`, not a blocking-grade signature (Finding 3).

4. **Registry kept only where it pays:** as the config seam for the deterministic checks and the named future case (project-invariant compliance). No generic AGENT-exec orchestrator, no per-dimension LLM plurality, no router, no coherence pass — until data from stage 2 proves the holistic pass actually misses something a split would catch.

5. **The primary success metric is FNR on a seeded known-bad-plan set**, with cost p50/p95 budgeted and capped per plan, before this is allowed to block anything.

**What's genuinely right:** the async-review / fast-signature-check split (latency tax lands once); the fail-open *invariant* (correct instinct, just needs the coverage-honesty fix); grounding judgment in sound external signal rather than introspection (§10E); the three-valued verdict with abstention; and the §10A LLM-Modulo floor (block-on-sound / advise-on-judgment). The research is excellent. The error is letting a 30-dimension registry, an FP-suppression contract, and a flat fan-out grow on top of conclusions that argue for the opposite of all three.
